title: Repair canonical tx_id collisions and rebuild invalid-seq reporting mismatch
summary: Repair the canonical-history damage caused by treating user-controlled ticket identifiers as canonical transaction identifiers, and fix the rebuild/reporting mismatch that currently reports `rebuild_invalid_seq` as the last valid sequence rather than the invalid event sequence in some drift cases. This ticket shifts the repair model toward opaque database-style `tx_id` values, keeps human-facing ticket naming separate from canonical transaction identity, and restores trustworthy canonical observability before further lifecycle automation depends on it.
outputs: A documented repair plan for canonical tx_id collisions caused by using user-controlled ticket names as canonical identifiers
Implementation changes that report invalid event sequence metadata consistently during rebuild drift detection
Guidance and validation for opaque database-style canonical transaction identifiers
Regression tests covering duplicate-begin collision reporting and invalid-sequence metadata consistency
acceptance_criteria: The ticket explicitly identifies user-controlled ticket naming being reused as canonical `tx_id` as a root cause of duplicate `tx.begin` drift and treats it separately from helper-bootstrap guard work.
Rebuild/reporting output does not contradict itself: when an invalid event is known, the reported invalid sequence matches that invalid event rather than a different last-valid sequence value.
The implementation or documented contract makes it clear that canonical `tx_id` values should be opaque database-style identifiers rather than user-controlled version names or ticket labels.
Repair guidance distinguishes between historical log repair, future tx_id-generation rules, and runtime duplicate-begin prevention so those concerns are not conflated.
Regression tests fail if rebuild drift output reports mismatched invalid-event and invalid-sequence metadata for the same failure.
Regression tests or documented checks cover tx_id collision scenarios where distinct releases reuse the same human ticket label such as `p2-t01`.
tasks: Document the observed canonical collision pattern where user-controlled ticket labels such as `p2-t01` were reused and later replay treated the newest `tx.begin` as a duplicate because `tx_id` was not an opaque unique key.
Audit rebuild drift reporting so invalid-event metadata, invalid-sequence metadata, and observed-mismatch summaries all refer to the same offending event when available.

Decide and document the canonical identifier rule for future work: `tx_id` should be generated as an opaque database-style identifier, while user-facing ticket labels remain separate descriptive fields.

Determine the safest repair strategy for existing damaged history, including whether the system should rely on explicit operator repair guidance, derived mapping, or replacement transactions with fresh opaque `tx_id` values rather than pretending the old history is healthy.
Add or update tests for duplicate-begin collision scenarios and for the invalid-seq reporting mismatch in rebuild drift output.
Ensure any runtime or documentation updates preserve strict canonical ordering and do not silently mask tx_id collisions as healthy resumable state.
notes: This ticket is about canonical-history repairability and observability accuracy, not just about preventing one more helper-side duplicate begin.

The root cause includes using user-controlled labels as canonical identifiers, so the fix must separate opaque `tx_id` generation from human-facing ticket naming in addition to any code-level drift reporting change.

The known reporting bug is that rebuild logic can retain the invalid event itself while later overwriting `rebuild_invalid_seq` with the last valid sequence, which makes diagnostics misleading during incident triage.

This ticket should complement p2-t06: p2-t06 hardens helper bootstrap behavior, while p2-t07 repairs the diagnostic and operational clarity around historical collisions, opaque tx_id discipline, and rebuild reporting.
Successful completion should make it much easier to distinguish a real duplicate-begin caused by tx_id collision from a misleading reporting artifact in rebuild output.

Progress update: opaque `tx_id` generation is now separated from user-controlled ticket labels at runtime lifecycle entry points, and rebuild/reporting guidance has been clarified for collision handling.

Historical repair guidance must stay explicit: damaged logs that reused a human ticket label as canonical `tx_id` should be treated as requiring operator-visible repair or replacement with fresh opaque transaction identifiers, not silently normalized as healthy history.
Regression coverage now includes invalid-sequence reporting consistency and release-to-release ticket-label collision scenarios so canonical `tx_id` values remain unique and opaque.
